name: Front Matter and Here Be Legends

pages.name: p. 1 - A L P H A  V . 1 . 2

text.content:
Original PDF page 1

A L P H A  V . 1 . 2

Original PDF page 2

Alpha Document!

Hello there! Just a reminder that this is an ALPHA
document for Ariadne’s Book of Legends. Anything you
see here is subject to change, from the mechanics to the
art, formatting, and even the lore. In fact, our goal is to
improve greatly upon what you see here, with your
priceless help! If all goes as planned, the final product
will look fairly different.

In this document, you’ll see us, Alvar and Marcelo,
creative leads of the Codex of Strings, doing what we’ve
been doing since the beginning: a bold digital release
with innovative ideas that sometimes work… and
sometimes don’t. Much of the book’s art remains in
production, so many pages might look like big ol’ blocks
of text. If you wish to see a finished product, this Alpha
release is not for you! That is alright. Not everyone plays
games in Early Access, and we certainly do not expect
you to do that. But if you do, we thank you, from the
bottom of our hearts, for helping us construct this
enormous project!

Why an Alpha?

Our digital delivery date is months away. Why put out
something incomplete this early? Truth be told, this is
our first Kickstarter, and we jumped headfirst into a
complex, multi-edition project that deals with what has
historically been the most neglected, least-balanced tier
of D&D–high-level play. Cheers to us!

We want your help to ensure the final book is
reflective of our backers’ particular wants and needs.
The more eyes we have on our mechanics, the better.
This is not akin to playtesting a new monster or a new
subclass; each individual table might have a completely
different idea of what Legendary Levels entail. Some
people have been playing beyond 20th level for decades
already!

So, if you’d be so kind, we’d love for you to play
around with the contents of this book and genuinely try
to break it as much as possible. Stress-test it!

Read at your Own Pace

The Book of Legends isn’t exactly a beginner-friendly
book. Take your time to understand what exactly the
Legendary Levels mean for your table. With this in
mind, if you’re ever not sure of how a specific feature or
ability works, tell us! It might mean we need to clarify it
for the final release.

ALPHA DOCUMENT

What is Useful Feedback?

Reading through this book is swell and all, but we are
particularly interested in your playtesting experience.
Many features, spells, and abilities present in this book
can seem absolutely bonkers at first glance, only to not
be that useful once they are put into play. There are
many moving parts to this grand work of ours, and we
are more interested in the interactions between
systems, characters, and foes than in the minutiae of a
certain feature on paper.

Something quite useful might look like this:

My players and I ran a combat encounter against Vosculd
the Avenger, and the Wizard absolutely smoked him on
round 3 by using their Heroic Path feature along with their
10th-level spell to deal over 750 points of damage. Though
it was hard to pull off and required the entire party to
cooperate, I’m pretty sure that was not an intended
interaction between those two features.

This is great feedback; it explains the situation that led
to the overpowered interaction and allows us to
evaluate whether or not that should still be included in
our book. An example of something less useful would
look like this:

The Rogue’s 25th-level feature is absolutely overpowered.

This lacks context! Indeed, the Rogue’s final ability
might look quite powerful, but compared to other 25thlevel features, it seems to be in line. Remember, the
whole point of this book is going above and beyond!
Things will jump out to you as incredibly powerful, on
both sides of the DM screen.

Click Here or scan the QR code to

leave your feedback!

Original PDF page 3

Ariadne’s Book of Legends © 2026 by Ariadne’s Codex of Strings LLC.
All rights reserved. Ariadne’s Codex of Strings is based in Lewes,
Delaware, USA

The content here contains references to Wizards of the Coast’s 5e SRD,
and is indeed shaped for compatibility with the rules and content there
described. Everything we use from the SRD belongs solely to WoTC
and Hasbro: we offer an expansion upon that content, by the terms of
the CC-BY-4.0​ license under which the SRD is published.

As a paying customer of Ariadne’s Codex of Strings LLC, whether via a
direct website purchase or Patreon subscription, you are allowed to
remix, adapt, and build upon the material in any medium or format, so
long as attribution is given to “Ariadne’s Codex of Strings”. You may not
distribute the content as-is: it must be transformed, or used within a
context in which our original content makes up no more than 20% of the
total new production.

The initial formatting of this Alpha is made using the Hombrewery
software by NaturalCrit, licensed under an MIT license. As such, we
express our profound thanks to them for providing this wonderful tool,
and we hereby recommend it to anyone in the process of making
professional-looking homebrew content for TTRPGs!

On The Cover…

In this piece by Ignacio Segesso, Ariadne Stringweaver
confronts Theseus, the Lightslayer, along with her party
of heroes. This climactic encounter has been centuries
in the making, as the two once-lovers who defeated the
Minotaur now battle over the fate of the cosmos.
Contact Information

You may email us at support@codexofstrings.com if you
have any questions, collaboration proposals, or, truth be
told, anything you want to tell us at all!

If you want a great deal on all our publications, find us
on Patreon and subscribe to one of our amazing tiers!
You can also follow us on Instagram to keep track of
our latest stuff! For a full list of where to find us, check
out our planar travel hub at www.codexofstrings.com,
which will direct you anywhere you need!

PDF Recommendations

Hey you! For the best PDF experience, we
recommend viewing this document in two-page
layout with “Show cover page separately” enabled.
This is the intended way to read the book. We
recommend using Microsoft Edge to view this
PDF, as it rarely glitches and will optimize render
time and memory usage.

Broken PDFs! Using an Apple device? The PDF
might be rendering poorly. Images over text, gray
boxes around notes like this one, missing images;
this is an issue some iOS devices experience with
image-heavy PDFs. Sometimes, even an iOS update
can break PDFs that previously rendered without
issue! Using a different PDF reader, such as
Microsoft Edge, Google Drive, or Adobe
Acrobat Reader usually solves this issue.

If you are having any problems, please contact us at
support@codexofstrings.com and we’ll help!

Original PDF page 6

HERE BE LEGENDS

Original PDF page 7

An Introduction:
Here Be Legends

 
W

elcome, hero, to your final ascent! What

follows is the culmination of a decade’s

worth of playing beyond 20th level,
spanning several campaigns, previous
Codex of Strings releases, and, thanks to

our wonderful backers, a successfully
funded Kickstarter. Now we take our very
best shot at making this playstyle, so dear to our hearts,

easier to access, more enjoyable to play, and simpler to

run.

The information in this Alpha is intended to give you a
complete framework of our Legendary System, allowing

players to reach up to 25th level and giving DMs the
tools to orchestrate a balanced and fun game for parties

of such might. You’ll find rules and advice for roleplay,
exploration, combat, and every other component of this

game we know and love!
There are several works out there that tackle
Legendary Play, both homebrew and official. Ariadne’s

Book of Legends is inspired by the greats that came

before, from the Epic Level Handbook to the Epic
Destinies of 4th edition and the Epic Boons of 5e and

5.5e; even the old Immortals ruleset of 1st edition.

We’ve chosen to tread our own path, creating an
overarching system that can be laid over the current
D&D framework, focusing on turn simplicity, cinematic

moments, and cosmic showdowns.

Thus, our Legendary Tiers will take you through heights

yet unknown, a truly special experience to have in a 5e

or 5.5e game. Perhaps you are a veteran of epic-level
play, finding difficulty in the great balance that must be

struck between overpowered parties and ludicrously
difficult encounters. Perhaps you are new to this world,

having joined our Kickstarter project with the hope of

one day playing through these impossible levels.

Welcome, I say again, to the Book of Legends. We
hope, through these pages, to channel the wonder

Legendary Play can bring to the fantasy TTRPG
experience. You need not be a veteran D&D player or a

number-crunching wargamer to enjoy yourself; you
need only the willingness to go beyond all limits and
weave a myth that will be remembered forevermore.

Oh, you’re here, at last! You’ve traveled
far, no doubt, in search of the legendary

powers bequeathed to the Ascended. Get

ready! The Strings of Fate have
something in store for you–I can feel it!

– Ariadne Stringweaver

HERE BE LEGENDS

Original PDF page 8

A Legendary Campaign

Why would you ever play beyond 15th level? That is a
question many have asked us, as we explained our
grandiose plans for this project. Most D&D stories end
way before even the third tier of play. Spells of 7th level
and above are a thing of bad guys much more than of
players; features become too hard to balance, and
encounters swing wildly between too easy and decidedly
unfair.

This is the verdict you’ll find online, and it may
discourage you from ever trying to run 5e at higher
levels.

Yet, there are those first-time DMs–perhaps having
awarded too many levels during the first handful of
sessions–that continue running their games well beyond
what is “recommended”, and somehow, they make it
work. As the players grow stronger and the 5e monsters
become less capable of posing a challenge to them, such
DMs begin inventing stat blocks, increasing hit points to
match damage outputs, and countering the party’s main
abilities with creations of their own. Magic items begin
occupying the party’s inventory, creating unexpected,
game-breaking interactions. Artifacts increase a
character’s power level until they become part of the
balancing of future encounters. Homebrew progression
systems give abilities way beyond the party’s level after
completing key story events. This group might have
been killing rats during their first session. They now
defy the very Gods.

And as long as the group keeps playing, the stakes
grow ever-higher, the levels increase, and the characters
transcend well beyond what the core rules were
designed for. Gaining a level becomes irrelevant
compared to discovering an artifact or unlocking a new
homebrew ability. This group has long left D&D behind
to create their own TTRPG, a special, unique
combination of impossibly powerful items, absurd
homebrew abilities, and, if you’re like us, a bunch of
anime references.

This transformation, this ascent beyond the rules of
D&D, happens to many who simply never stop playing
their favorite campaign. The balance that is struck
between players who know exactly how their characters
work and a DM accustomed to challenging them is
something truly magical. And it is unbalanced, and
goofy, and requires constant maintenance behind the
scenes. But it is worth it.

You don’t need this book to play Legendary D&D. You
need only a steadfast will, a bunch of homebrew, and
time on the weekend. You will learn as you play, and
perhaps, some encounters will be too easy, and some
too hard. They will be memorable either way. We are
here to make this process smoother, simpler, and
decidedly epic. But we began just like you–a group of
friends who continued to play, well into the “hard
levels” of D&D, simply because we were having
too much fun.

HERE BE LEGENDS

 
Legendary Players

The 20th level is the absolute limit for 99% of heroes;
the constraints of a mortal body are hard to surpass,
after all. Hard, but not impossible. When an
exceptionally talented individual is blessed with
unyielding determination and great teachers, they might
just rise above to become legendary. The highest
achievers among all mortalkind, comparable in power
to demigods and immortals.

A barbarian bearing the weight of the world upon
their shoulders. A wizard with such command over the
forces of reality that they can evoke miniature stars to
vaporize their enemies. A brilliant paladin blessed by
their God, fearlessly leading the charge against the
darkest forces in existence. These are all examples of
what it means to be a legendary character. Not just a
marginal improvement, but a greater dimension of
power, available only to the greatest of their generation.

Thematically, legendary characters are individuals
who, through rigorous training and peerless talent, have
achieved significant breakthroughs in their respective
fields. They can do things that seem impossible to their
lesser counterparts–which is to say, nearly everyone.
Mechanically, this means Legendary Characters break a
lot of D&D’s golden rules. Staples of balancing, such as
casting one leveled spell per turn, go out the window.

This is a design choice. Legendary characters are
meant to do the impossible, not just be marginally better
than their non-legendary counterparts.

Original PDF page 9

Legendary Challenges
The Universe of Strings

The stories told beyond 20th level deal with shattering
realities, planar adventures, and world-ending threats.
Players are akin to superheroes, anime protagonists,
and mythical demigods. The stakes must be raised to
their level.

In your world, there may be great creatures that hide
within the darkness of the cosmos, monsters so terrible
that the mere mention of them turns valiant heroes into
cowards. There may be guardians, ancient and divine,
that protect realms beyond reality from forces much
older than mortalkind. And there may be Gods, playing
the long game of divinity. Some lay amid the stars,
entities whose breath can reshape worlds, create
species, bring about cataclysms. Some choose to
meddle, to play with the younger races, raise them up,
only to discard them when they grow bored. And some
yet remain asleep, kept in slumber by ancient seals and
praying paragons, with the knowledge that their waking
would bring about a grand reckoning.

In a Legendary Game, the chains binding these
cosmic beasts are brittle. The greed of the planar lords
is great. The power of Divinity is tempting. Conflict
inundates the firmament, and heroes must rise to quell
the flames of war–or to bask in their warmth.

Challenges that come to rely on Legendary
Characters may seem impossible to overcome. A
campaign set mainly in our Legendary Tier of play will
often deal with divine gambits, apocalyptic scenarios,
and reality-breaking
 magic. We stand in the
battleground of
 devils, angels, and
Gods, and the
 characters,
together, can
 face divinity.

The backdrop for this manual is the Universe of Strings,
our original, sometimes-dark, sometimes-heroic fantasy
setting in which divinity has long perished, leaving the
dregs of godhood for anyone to take. Mortals,
primordials, celestials, and demons fight over Threads
of Divinity, essences that take many forms, but always
allow one to embody a divine domain that once
belonged to the Pantheon. Though this book is mainly
focused on the mechanics behind Legendary Play, this
story inspires every spell, mythical technique, and
legendary foe described in these pages.

This shattered, anarchic cosmos of fallen stars and
dead paragons has as much darkness as it does light,
filled with messianic heroes of all creeds who strive to
make their abandoned worlds a better place, but also
with villains content with exploiting what is broken.
Legendary stories are commonplace in such a setting,
where powers of many sources choose opposing vessels
and go to war, even thousands of years after the Fall of
the Gods. And over them all, the Void watches; Eternity,
the Great Enemy who slew the Gods, looking to
extinguish the dying light of creation once and for all.

As you shift through the pages of this book, you will
find tidbits of information about these tales, from the
lore behind ancient artifacts to the stories of the foes
that stand ready to face your Legendary Party. You can
incorporate these stories into your game, or even adopt
the Universe of Strings’ setting altogether, if you want.
It is, in our style, extremely well suited to Legendary
Play! If you want to know more about this setting, we
recommend you check out our SRD document. It’s a
free download on our website! Click here to visit.

Your Own Legendary Campaign
If you don’t want to adopt anything from the Universe of
Strings, do not fret! This book is also for you. All the
mechanical content is either plug-and-play or very easily
adaptable to any setting based on D&D mechanical and
narrative conventions. After all, we want you to play your
version of Legendary Tier TTRPGs, and we realize that,
more often than not, that means playing with your
established cosmology, characters, and magic systems!

Also, if you play any flavor of dark or heroic fantasy at
your table, you’ll find the stories and lore within this book
and our SRD very easy to drag-and-drop into your own
setting. Always remember… good artists copy, great artists
steal!

HERE BE LEGENDS

Original PDF page 10

Using this Book

To play a Legendary Campaign, you must first be very
familiar with your chosen edition (5e or 5.5e). Though
our main focus is to simplify the D&D high-level
experience with our system additions, the tales you will
weave far exceed the complexity of your regular 5th to
10th level adventure. Your arsenal will increase in
scope, both as a player and as a DM, and you must be
prepared to wield such powers.

Legendary Tiers of Play

A Legendary Campaign is separated into three
Legendary Tiers of play. Each of these tiers is a
magnitude greater than the last, with a pronounced
difference in power level between them.

Challenge Rating on our Legendary Foes has been
replaced by a Recommended Level, ranging from
20th to 25th. These correspond to each of the three
tiers; Legend (20th - 21st), Paragon (22nd - 23rd), or
Ascended (24th - 25th). Each tier is of great importance
when building a Legendary Campaign, and using all
three properly creates a believable power scaling
structure, even beyond the heights of 20th level.

Gameplay-wise, each Legendary Tier is a mighty
jump from the last, increasing the Legendary Points
of both players and foes. This means a Paragon-level
stat block is intended to contend with 4-5 characters
with 2 Legendary Points each, which gives them access
to many more resources than a Legend-level party
might possess.

LP Progression

Tier
Characters
Foes

Legend (20-21)
1 LP
3 LP

Paragon (22-23)
2 LP
4 LP

Ascended (24-25)
3 LP
5 LP

Modular Difficulty. Each Legendary Tier is comprised
of 2 levels. A party on the lower level will find the
challenge posed by a Legendary Foe of that tier to be
hard but fair, while a party on the higher level will find
the challenge to be balanced and surmountable.

Proficiency Bonus

Players increase their proficiency bonus each time they
reach a new tier of play, while Legendary Foes remain
at a proficiency bonus of +10 for all three tiers.

Legends (Levels 20 - 21)

The first tier of Legendary Play we offer in this book
includes a buffed version of 20th level, as players gain
access to Legendary Points, their associated Legendary
Resistance, and one class-specific feature. At the
Legend tier, characters take their first step beyond the
limits of mortals. Their names carry weight across

HERE BE LEGENDS

kingdoms and planar domains, and through their deeds,
nations rise or fall. A 20+ level hero is a living myth,
possibly the greatest living practitioner of their specific
class.

This tier is about slaying primordial monsters,
mending planar rifts, rewriting the fate of empires, and
other adventures that are just a touch beyond the fourth
tier of play outlined in the DM’s Guide. Legend-tier play
demands world-shaking stakes and adversaries to
match– ancient dragons, demon lords, and interplanar
criminals.
Paragons (Levels 22 - 23)

Paragons transcend even legend, becoming the greatest
heroes in recorded history. Their actions echo across
planes, and cosmic powers take notice. They are known
beyond the confines of their plane as famous people of
their world, like a mortal may know the names of
storied angels or devils. A 22nd-level hero might be the
most powerful practitioner of their class in centuries.

This tier is about becoming a force in the cosmos.
Paragon play shifts toward fighting Gods, toppling
demonic hierarchies, and ruling over planes of
existence. The Foes in this tier might be some of the
most powerful creatures in the Universe.
Ascended (Levels 24 - 25)

Ascended heroes stand on the threshold of divinity.
Some might already carry with them shards of Gods,
mortals might speak their names in prayer, and deities
might consider them equals, or at the very least, threats.
They have joined the Pantheon, if not in name, at least
in might. Their struggles are about dominion over
creation and the shaping of existence.

Ascended play is the culmination of an Era–claiming
a divine spark, forging a new plane, or choosing what
the next age of the world will become. This tier is
mythic in scope: battles in the Astral Sea, wars against
overdeities, and finales that determine the fate of
billions.

Balance of Players
We have designed and playtested this book for a party of 4-5
players. Some D&D groups have more, some have less.
Each Legendary Character added to the roster beyond these
numbers is a multiplying force that brings additional 10thlevel spells, mythical techniques, and magical items into the
fray, greatly tipping the scales of combat, exploration, and
roleplay alike. Fewer players, in turn, make the challenges
much harder to accomplish, though this is more easily
adjusted than a surplus of characters.

More information about how to handle non-standard
player counts against Legendary Foes on Chapter V.

Original PDF page 11

Progression

Unlike their non-legendary counterparts, characters
beyond 20th level do not simply advance in their
progression for virtue of completing an adventure or
gaining enough experience. Each legendary level is an
enormous hurdle to overcome, one that some people
simply cannot ever surpass. Many epic NPCs have
reached the heights of their potential, the absolute limit
of their growth, somewhere beyond 17th level. Some
may have trained for decades, even centuries, to gain a
single Legendary Level. The characters, the true heroes
of the story, have infinite potential, of course… but each
level beyond the 20th must be earned through feats
unparalleled. Legendary Levels are awarded at the
DM’s discretion, in moments that align with the game’s
narrative.

Any level beyond the 20th is a good place to stop,
depending on the scope of your game and the
willingness of your DM. Just because there are 25 levels
doesn’t mean you are obligated to reach them. Indeed,
parties of the Ascended tier struggle to find much
challenge in anything beyond the most powerful
Legendary Foes ever conceived.

Final Boss. The DM might have selected a specific
Legendary Foe as the final encounter of the campaign.
Depending on which tier the foe is from, it can mark the
expected ceiling of the adventure. An Ascended-tier
boss, recommended for levels 24 and 25, can be used to
fight a 23rd-level party, as a way to make the final battle
feel even more deadly. In fact, depending on the number
of players at your table, it may be wise to bump up the
difficulty of some of these battles to maintain tension.

20th-Level Adventure. Some stories never go beyond
20th level. Perhaps your campaign is nearing the final
stretch, and this book is here only to guide you through
high-level combat and milestones the players can
achieve for the final battle. Heroic Paths, Mythical
Techniques, 10th-level spells, and Legendary Class
features can serve to inspire a non-Legendary
Campaign, giving specific power-ups as boons from
Gods or features from lost artifacts without the
framework of Legendary Levels. Using a Legend-tier
Foe (recommended for 20th-21st level) as a Final Boss
against a properly equipped 17th or 18th level party is a
valid way of playing a Legendary Campaign! This
manual is here to inspire, and only you know what is
best for your specific table; no need to follow every rule!

Leveling Up

If you are expecting to level up regularly during your
campaign, we recommend that each increase in power
be marked by a Legendary Achievement, along the lines
of the following examples.

External. The character discovers a powerful relic,
inherits the powers of a legendary foe, or is blessed by a
God. These power increases are often the culmination
of an adventure–an external, immediate increase in

power gained through the adoption of a boon that exists
in your world. These are the best ways to level up a
Legendary Party–having that increase in power be
extraordinary, not the result of simple training and
adventuring.

Internal. The character hones their craft, whatever that
may be, which results in an increase in level. An
archmage discovers new spells beyond 9th level, a
sword saint masters Mythical Techniques lost to
memory and just recently recovered, a monk learns to
walk on air. These are often accompanied by some
external stimuli–discovering an ancient text depicting a
10th-level spell, for example–but occur internally and
take time and effort to complete. The first few
Legendary Levels are best for this kind of level-up.
Internal increases must always have a built-in reason to
occur! A constant of effort, dedication, and training on
the part of the character.

Downtime. Over several years, decades, or even
centuries of downtime, a Legendary Character may
perform mythical acts that increase their level. They
may have completed legendary adventures that will go
down in history as folk tales, discovered new laws of
reality in their isolated towers, or perfected their
techniques beyond their previous limits. These
moments between adventures are perfect for a
Legendary Character to increase their level.

Maximum Level

If a party ever reaches 25th level, it becomes very hard
to challenge the players with anything short of a God.
Even in this book, the creatures that stand ready to face
such a group are the minority. We recommend only
hitting max level in the last arc, in the epic ramp-up
toward the campaign finale. 25th level is, to this book,
what 20th level is for regular D&D!

Edition Compatibility
This book is written for both 5e and 5.5e. Terminology
generally follows 5.5e convention for player-facing rules,
while monster stat blocks lean closer to classic 5e design.

Where the two editions differ, the text will note which rule
applies to which version. Watch out for 5.5e or 5e
paragraphs, noted by their edition in bold letters. Some
features only exist in one edition–the increasing Rage count
for the Barbarian, for example, only matters in 5.5e, since 5e
Barbarians have an unlimited number of rages at 20th level.

Certain terms may be unfamiliar depending on which
edition you play. The Bloodied condition, for instance, is a
5.5e term referring to a creature that has fewer than half its
total hit points remaining.

Original PDF page 12

Summary

This book is divided into three great parts that include
multiple chapters each:

+

The introductory section you are reading now,
which settles the bases for a Legendary Campaign and
explains the overarching systems and philosophy that
permeate the book.

The Player section, from Chapter I to Chapter IV, will
give players the tools to create legendary characters.
+

+

The DM section, from Chapter IV to Chapter VIII, lists
the many monsters, relics, and additional information
a director needs to run a Legendary Campaign.

An Introduction: Here be Legends

This section contains general and special rules, along
with our design philosophy and recommendations to run
and play a Legendary Campaign.
Chapter I: Legendary Characters

This chapter outlines the legendary level progression (2025) for each of the 12 core classes of D&D 5e / 5.5e.
Soon, the Shaper and Starblade, our original classes, will
be added to the list.
Chapter II: Heroic Paths

This chapter introduces you to Heroic Paths, a
progression system in which you pick from a wide
selection of powerful feats, allowing you to customize
your Legendary Character. These feats are organized in
paths, which work somewhat like subclasses, with each
feature building upon previous ones.
Chapter III: Mythical Techniques

Martial classes have long been outscaled by learned
wizards, ancient druids, and ascendant sorcerers,
especially in the higher levels of D&D. No longer! This
chapter focuses on Mythical Techniques, powerful
abilities unlocked at certain legendary levels that further
help you customize your martial character, allowing them
to compete with the incredible might of epic magic.
Chapter IV: Epic Magic

Magic beyond 9th level is the ultimate show of power.
Both characters and foes will find themselves using their
legendary resources to conjure the most powerful
arcanisms in existence: 10th-level spells. This chapter
lists all spells we’ve created to accompany our legendary
system of play.
Chapter V: The Legendary Game

In all our years playing epic-level D&D, we’ve accrued
some good know-how about directing such mythical
games. This chapter will be your guide on maintaining
tension, running encounters challenging enough for
Legendary Characters, and preparing the stakes for the
world-shattering battles that will take place in your world.

HERE BE LEGENDS

Chapter VI: Legendary Foes

A campaign is nothing without its antagonist. This
chapter is focused on our menagerie of Legendary Foes,
creatures beyond CR 30 that have been specifically
designed to challenge even the most powerful of parties.
Lore, mechanics, lairs, and DM notes, this chapter will
teach you to run these foes at your table.
Chapter VII: Relics (Not In Alpha)

There are items more powerful than artifacts in the
cosmos. This chapter will describe relics that can be
acquired after defeating each of our Legendary Foes,
along with some additional iconic magic items from the
Universe of Strings.
Chapter VIII: Appendices (Not In
Alpha)

This final chapter will contain additional lore, easy-toreference tables, generic monsters for each Legendary
Tier, and additional tools to help you homebrew your
own Legendary Content.

Original PDF page 13

General Rules

D&D famously has three pillars of play: Combat,
Exploration, and Roleplay. In a Legendary Campaign,
the fundamentals of Exploration and Roleplay remain
mostly the same. Instead of exploring abandoned
dungeons, the party is delving into the castle of a
conquering lich, and instead of negotiating with the
town’s blacksmith, the players find themselves bartering
with Gods. Not only do these elements remain
engaging, but they’re arguably at their very best.
Combat, on the other hand, needs some work. We found
that never-ending turns, save-or-die mechanics, and
balancing issues are the main pain points for Legendary
Campaigns.

The following general rules are focused on all three
pillars, but mainly give a framework to improve combat.
To that end, we’ve made some fundamental
modifications to 5e / 5.5e, which tackle the problems
that typically make high-level play sloggish and unfun in
the base game.

Legendary Point System

The cornerstone of our take on high-level D&D is the
addition of Legendary Points, or LP for short. Both the
party and their adversaries will have a set number of
Legendary Points, which they can use to fuel certain
abilities.

Legendary Points (and their uses) come into play at
20th level.

LP Restriction

LP use has only one restriction. When a creature uses
an ability that expends LP, it cannot use that same
ability again until the start of its next turn. This goes for
players and monsters!

Recover Action

To regain spent LP, a character must use a new tool in
their arsenal: the Recover action. When a character
takes the Recover action, they regain all expended LP.

Legendary Foes, for their part, recover all their LP
automatically at the start of each of their turns.

Legendary Features

Classes and Foes have some features to spend LP on.
Let’s discuss some of them.

Legendary Resistance

When you fail a saving throw, you can expend 1 Legendary
Point to succeed instead.

At 20th level, to mark just how powerful the characters
have become—and to have a shot at defeating our
Legendary Foes—every class gains access to the
Legendary Resistance feature. This is a much-needed
addition, allowing us to still use the powerful spells and
abilities of high-level D&D without the fear of save-ordie mechanics.

Because LPs serve to fuel your best abilities in these
legendary levels, expending a Legendary Resistance
means not using a certain offensive capability, and
overusing your LPs on dealing damage, for example,
can leave you vulnerable to retaliation. We’ve found it to
be a dynamic game of risk/reward, which turns a fairly
arbitrary, bland feature like Legendary Resistances into
a fun mechanic that both DM and players interact with.

Multiplier Attacks

Special actions and attacks that deal high amounts of
damage use a multiplier instead of a set number of dice.
Most of these attacks are fueled by LP and are called
Multiplier Attacks.

Depending on the class or creature, they deal a
number of dice times 5 or 10. The damage output of a
Multiplier Attack is around 100 to 250 points of
damage, with some exceptions going higher or lower.
Multiplier attacks are epic, effective ways for characters
and creatures to deal damage in a simple, easy-tocalculate manner.

Limitations. Multiplier attacks follow certain caveats.
Multiplier Attacks do not benefit from any bonus to their
damage beyond their calculation. A critical hit from a
Multiplier Attack never duplicates the damage
calculation, but rather increases it by a set number of
dice, specified in each Multiplier Attack. Any effects that
would allow a creature to reroll damage dice do not
apply to Multiplier Attacks.

You can still do the math!
Some tables prefer rolling ludicrous amounts of dice once
in a while. You can always change the multiplier equations
for a set number of dice; for example, the Perfect Strike
Fighter feature deals 5d8 x 5 points of damage. That’s 25d8,
if you prefer rolling!

HERE BE LEGENDS

Original PDF page 14

Mythical Techniques

Martials also gain access to certain Mythical
Techniques, some of which require LP. These powerful
actions allow them to act outside of their turn, deal
incredible damage, or invoke a certain unique effect.

5.5e: Some classes gain additional Weapon Masteries
whenever they gain a Mythical Technique.

Class Features

Classes gain special ways of spending LP. These are
generally more situational, but remain powerful and
unique. Most of the time, Legendary Resistance and
Multiplier Attacks will be a more effective way of
spending LP during combat. These class features
change the way a character interacts with the world
around them, and can also change the tide of battle if
used at the proper time. When they hit, they really hit.

10th-level spells

Full casters gain access to legendary 10th-level spells,
arcanisms of such magnitude that they alter the very
fabric of reality. To cast a 10th-level spell, a caster must
use their Action on their turn and expend 2 Legendary
Points. This is the most LP-intensive action a character
can take, and leaves the casters with much fewer
options until they find a way to recover LP.

Legendary Foes follow the same rules and sometimes
have additional restrictions when it comes to casting
mighty 10th-level spells.

Legendary Foes

The LP System also applies to our Legendary Foes and
some of their minions. Legendary Points are used to
take Extra Actions, use Legendary Resistances and
Endurances, and perform especially powerful abilities
during their turn.

Unless otherwise stated, Legendary Foes must also
contend with the same restriction as the characters
when casting 10th-level spells, and sometimes have
additional restrictions, such as phase transitions,
casting times, and material components.

As a DM, be very transparent with the amount of LP
the Foes have at any given moment; your players must
be able to strategize depending on what the villain is
capable of.

Legendary Recovery. At the start of each of the
Legendary Foe’s turns, it regains all spent LP.

HERE BE LEGENDS

 
LP outside of Combat

Think about Legendary Points as a measure of a
legendary character’s basic power. Just as a Fighter
might attack or a Warlock cast an eldritch blast, a
Legendary Fighter can cut reality apart, and a
Legendary Warlock can shoot several beams of
overwhelming power effortlessly. This applies to
Legendary Resistances, too, meaning a stray Wisdom
saving throw outside of combat can easily be resisted by
a Legendary Character, needing only the next 6 seconds
to recover and be able to do it again.

As long as a character can take a round to recover,
they are always capable of legendary feats. That is what
separates a powerful 17th-level adventurer from a 20thlevel legend.

Roleplay and Exploration Features. Some classes
possess powerful features that allow them to create
effects more effective in roleplay and exploration
scenarios. Some spellcaster features allow them to
expend LP to use certain spells without expending a
spell slot, meaning they could always have a dimension
door at the ready, or the ability to heal another
character to full health, given enough minutes. Certain
Mythical Techniques give martials the tools to traverse
great distances, fly, or open planar rifts, all with the use
of Legendary Points.

During combat, some of these features become much
harder to use and are often ignored, yet in other aspects
of the game, they redefine the baseline capabilities a
Legendary Character possesses. The Hulk needs not
take a short rest after each Thunderclap, Goku can
always use his Instant Transmission, and Satoru Gojo
never finds himself without his impenetrable Infinity.

Infinite Legendary Resistances. The only restriction
on Legendary Resistances is having the LP to use them
and only using one per round. This means saving
throws outside of combat will always be succeeded by
Legendary Characters. A Dexterity saving throw to
avoid falling into a ravine, a Strength saving throw to
push through difficult traveling conditions, a
Constitution saving throw to avoid the paralysis of a
poison dart trap–these mundane challenges aren’t
obstacles for legends.

The limitations of your pitiful race
amuse me to no end. My blood bleeds
black, my eyes see shadow. Don’t you
know? I have transcended even my own
kin. I am the greatest Teng to have ever

lived!

– The Jester of the Moonsilver Court

Original PDF page 15

Difficulty Classes

There is beautiful math behind most interactions in the
5e and 5.5e system. Proficiency Bonus + Statistic
Modifier + Number = DC. This formula is good enough
for lower levels, but becomes quite cumbersome even
before reaching the Legendary Levels.

Characters will mostly retain these sorts of Difficulty
Class calculations, though some Mythical Techniques
and 10th-level Spells have their own DCs.

Legendary Foes will have a plethora of different
Difficulty Classes, depending on the feature that is being
used.

Traps, encounter-based DCs, and other such cases
should be determined on a case-by-case basis, much like
you would in a non-legendary campaign. Maximum
DCs, however, can easily reach beyond 30.

Legendary Difficulty Classes

Task Difficulty
DC

Exceedingly Difficult
30

Legendary Feat
35

Divine Act
40+

Keep in mind that some rolls, skill checks in particular,
can very well surpass 40, at which point the action may
break the bounds of traditional game logic. There can
always be situations that call for a DC 50 or 60 roll. Just
remember, the DM always has the final say.

Stealth DC 40: The Rogue walks the transitive
space between the material and ethereal dimensions,
becoming impossible to track, even through divine
means.

Athletics DC 50: The Barbarian lifts a mountain,
keeping it over their shoulders, like Atlas holding up the
world.

Performance DC 60: The Bard performs so
beautifully, stars begin to weep, falling from their orbit
and bombarding the material plane, if only to hear the
music a little closer.

Armor Class

Long before you reach 20th level, AC becomes
somewhat trivial. Plate armor gives an AC of 18
regardless of level, and AC increases depend too much
on magic items and spells, all of which become
available fairly early into your leveling journey. There
simply isn’t a compelling progression system when it
comes to armor class, and though it works quite well for
the first tiers of D&D, it falls short for our purposes.

Legendary AC. As a baseline, all legendary classes will
benefit from an AC equal to their level, should their AC
calculations be lower without this bonus. Certain
customizable options will allow builds that focus on AC
to reach ludicrous numbers, and some classes will gain
extra AC as they reach higher Legendary Levels.

At 20th level, if your AC calculation is lower than your level,
you can use your level as your AC instead. If you are using
your Legendary AC, you cannot further increase your AC.

One or the Other. If a player is choosing to benefit
from Legendary AC, they are forfeiting any and all
bonuses to their AC they would otherwise get, with the
exception of temporary magical effects. This means that
a Legendary Fighter who chooses to benefit from
Legendary AC does not increase their AC as per the
Fighter’s AC feature.

Some magical effects give temporary AC bonuses,
such as Haste, Shield, and Shield of Fate. When using
Legendary AC, these bonuses do apply. Permanent
magical bonuses, however, such as those given by a
magic item or a spell or effect lasting longer than 1
hour, do not apply to Legendary AC.

Legendary Damage Types

Several new damage types appear in this book,
exclusive to Legendary Foes and Characters.

Dark Damage. A blasphemous damage type appearing
as black streaks of energy. When a creature takes Dark
damage, its hit point maximum is reduced by the same
amount for 24 hours. This reduction can be lifted early
by Remove Curse, Greater Restoration, or a similar
effect. A creature reduced to 0 hit points by Dark
damage cannot be revived by any effect below 9th-level.
Creatures that are vulnerable to necrotic damage are
also vulnerable to Dark damage. This damage type
cannot be resisted by effects that guard against spells or
physical damage, only by features that explicitly grant
resistance to it or to all damage.

Lethal Damage. A magical form of physical damage,
appearing as a powerful red force around a weapon.
Creatures vulnerable to bludgeoning, piercing, or
slashing damage are also vulnerable to Lethal damage.
Lethal damage from critical hits deals triple damage
instead of double against creatures that are bloodied.
Lethal damage dealt by a Multiplier Attack does not
benefit from this triple damage, but instead increases
the Multiplier attack’s critical calculation by 1 additional
damage die; from 8d8 x 5 to 9d8 x5, for example.

Soul Damage. A fundamental form of damage
appearing as white flame. Soulless creatures–such as
constructs and certain corporeal undead–are immune to
it, at the DM’s discretion. Attacks or spell effects that
deal Soul Damage always affect creatures in the
ethereal plane and bypass temporary hit points. Soul
damage cannot be resisted or reduced by any means
short of having no soul to wound–this also means that,
even if a creature is immune to all damage as part of a
spell, feature, or artifact, Soul damage will still affect it.
 

HERE BE LEGENDS

Original PDF page 16

Action Priority

The many features present in this book make it possible
for several creatures to act outside of their turn
simultaneously. Extra actions, Legendary Actions,
Mythical Techniques, and certain class features can all
compete for the same window between turns.

When two or more creatures attempt to use
Legendary Actions or Extra actions at the end of the
same turn, resolve them in initiative order. A 25th-level
Fighter using their Legendary Action feature at the end
of another player’s turn, for example, will act before a
Legendary Foe’s Extra Action if the Fighter has the
higher initiative count–and after it if they do not.

Some Mythical Techniques and class features can
also be used outside of one’s turn by expending LP.
These are resolved after Extra actions and Legendary
Actions and follow the same initiative order priority.

Reactions and Phase Transitions are the exception to
this rule. They resolve immediately when triggered,
taking precedence over all other pending actions. If
multiple reactions or Phase Transitions are triggered at
the same time, resolve them in reverse order–the most
recently triggered effect resolves first.

Action Priority

Priority Action Type
Timing

1st
Reactions & Phase
Transitions

Immediately on trigger,
most recent first

2nd
Extra Actions &
Legendary Actions

In initiative order, highest
first

3rd
All other out-of-turn
features

In initiative order, highest
first

Incapacitations

Certain spells and abilities provide players with the
ability to “stunlock” the bad guy. Famously, a high-level
Monk can dish out enough stunning strikes to dazzle
any monster foolish enough not to have immunity to the
stunned condition. Casters can sling ten or so lowerlevel spells that are highly incapacitating, and will
destroy your action economy if they get through.
Something as simple as Sleep, Hideous Laughter, or
Slow can arrest the battle’s momentum for the price of
a low-level spell slot.

A single Legendary Foe can never be completely
neutralized, come their turn to play. Our solution is to
add fixes to both Characters and Foes that allow for a
certain bypass of condition immunities. Second, an
ability in every legendary foe’s toolkit to end stun-locks
and similar effects. Third, a way to reward Legendary
Characters for getting incapacitating effects through the
foe’s high saving throws without the save-or-suck
dynamics of D&D.

HERE BE LEGENDS

Limited Magic Immunity

[Legendary Foes] cannot be affected by spells cast at 3rd
level or lower unless they wish to be.

You’ll find this feature on every stat block in this book.
This lowers the amount of resources the casters
possess to neutralize a foe. If your sorcerer wants to
cast Slow, they will have to do so at 4th level!

Cleanse Effect

If the [Legendary Foe] starts its turn affected by a condition
or magical effect of 7th level or lower, it can expend 1
Legendary Point to end the effect. If the effect or condition
is of 8th or 9th level, or from a Mythical Technique, the
[Legendary Foe] must expend 3 Legendary Points to end
that effect. A Legendary Foe cannot end a magical effect
from a 10th-level spell.

This allows certain effects to still take root, but not to
the extent of neutralizing a Legendary Creature. Yes,
Hold Monster might get through, but the Legendary Foe
will be able to end the effect on its turn, at a cost. This
said, powerful spells and mythical techniques remain
incredibly meaningful, taking up most of a Legendary
Foe’s LP for the round.

Cleanse Effect is meant to end any effect or
incapacitation the foe might be experiencing, even if it
doesn’t fall into either the condition or magical effect
categories. For example, should a Foe be trapped under
a great heap of rubble, it can use a Legendary Point to
free itself. As the DM, use your judgment to determine
the extent of this feature.

Impaired

An Impaired creature cannot take actions that cost 3 or
more Legendary Points. The Impaired condition usually
lasts for either a round or a minute, or until a Legendary
Foe ends the condition by spending 1 Legendary Point at
the beginning of its turn.

To ensure the player’s tools are still useful but don’t bar
the Legendary Foes from acting, we created the
Impaired condition. In several Legendary Characters’
features, you’ll find the following wording.

If the target is immune to the […] condition, it is Impaired
instead.

Original PDF page 17

Magic

Magic and magical effects are the backbone of any
balanced campaign at higher levels. To help DMs and
Players alike gauge which spell would take precedence
over which other, or how a certain magical effect would
interact with an ongoing spell, we’ve created a scale of
magic to categorize the relative strength of magic in
your Legendary Campaign. It’s a simple enough system
that helps rule things on the fly and maintain
immersion.

Magical Effects. Many creatures and classes utilize
powerful effects that could be considered magic, yet
aren’t specifically leveled. To know how a Mythical
Technique or high-level Artifact would interact with
these features, the DM must assign a level to that
magical effect. Each level implies a different tier of
magic. If a feature in this book refers to a certain magic
tier, such as Common-tier, Higher-tier, or Epic-tier, it
refers to the level brackets mentioned below. These
Tiers of Magic allow you to judge whether or not
Scáthach’s Scythe of Doom could destroy a Force Cage,
for example (the answer is yes!).

Level
Tier
Spell Example
Effect Example

9thlevel

Simulacrum

Laws

Counterspell, Dispel Magic, End Magic, Cleanse
Effect. The characters and foes in high-level games
tend to have a lot of tools at their disposal when it
comes to ending magical effects, countering spells, and
interrupting casters.

With the higher ability scores this book allows,
countering a 9th-level spell with a 3rd-level casting of
Counterspell is not that difficult, and Dispel Magic, even
at 3rd-level, may very well end a character’s Foresight
spell. There is a discrepancy between the resource
expended and the gameplay effect.

Our solution is a new rule that affects not only the
Legendary Levels, but also the rest of the leveling
process (if you’re brave enough).

Tiers of Magic

A spell or magical effect can only modify, end, or otherwise
affect another spell or magical effect if it is from a tier of
magic equal to or higher than the target magic.

This mainly affects Counterspell and Dispel Magic, but
it can also be used to quantify and determine the
strength of any type of magic, such as the toughness of
an ancient barrier or the anti-magical properties of an
alien material.

End Magic and Cleanse Effect, features many
Legendary Foes possess, also follow this rule, needing
an increased spend in LP to affect greater levels of
magic.
